title: Taking Hold of Your Mind: “How” Skills Nonjudgmentally   See, but don’t evaluate as good or bad. Just the facts.

pages.text:
60
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
«
Mindfulness Handout 5   (Mindfulness Worksheets 2–2c, 5–5c)
Taking Hold of Your Mind: “How” Skills
Nonjudgmentally

 See, but don’t evaluate as good or bad. Just the facts.

 Accept each moment like a blanket spread out on the lawn, accepting both the rain and the
sun and each leaf that falls upon it.

 Acknowledge the difference between the helpful and the harmful, the safe and the dangerous,
but don’t judge them.

 Acknowledge your values, your wishes, your emotional reactions, but don’t judge them.

 When you find yourself judging, don’t judge your judging.
One-Mindfully

 Rivet yourself to now. Be completely present to this one moment.

 Do one thing at a time. Notice the desire to be half-present, to be somewhere else, to go
somewhere else in your mind, to do something else, to multitask—and then come back to one
thing at a time.
•• When you are eating, eat.
•• When you are walking, walk.
•• When you are worrying, worry.
•• When you are planning, plan.
•• When you are remembering, remember.

 Let go of distractions. If other actions, or other thoughts, or strong feelings distract you, go
back to what you are doing—again, and again, and again.

 Concentrate your mind. If you find you are doing two things at once, stop—go back to one
thing at a time (the opposite of multitasking!).
Effectively

 Be mindful of your goals in the situation, and do what is necessary to achieve them.

 Focus on what works. (Don’t let emotion mind get in the way of being effective.)

 Play by the rules.

 Act as skillfully as you can. Do what is needed for the situation you are in—not the situation
you wish you were in; not the one that is fair; not the one that is more comfortable.

 Let go of willfulness and sitting on your hands.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Taking Hold of Your Mind: “How” Skills Nonjudgmentally   See, but don’t evaluate as good or bad. Just the facts.
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Mindfulness is allowed to be tiny, sensory-safe, and done from bed or wheelchair. Noticing without judging counts even when the day is messy.
tiny_steps: Find one neutral thing in the room.
Return to the next safe action.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use eyes-open grounding if closing eyes feels unsafe.
Use quiet, low-scent, low-touch options for MCAS, trauma, and sensory overload.
No posture requirement; lying down or supported sitting is valid.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not force stillness when it increases pain, panic, dysphoria, or trauma activation.
